title: JavaScript Error Types
intro: JavaScript has several built-in error types and each one tells you something different about what went wrong. Just like warning lights on a car dashboard each point to a different system, each error type points to a different kind of fix. For each statement, swipe RIGHT if it's correct or LEFT if it's wrong.

teach.title: Error Types Are Like Medical Symptoms
teach.body: When you go to a doctor, they don't just say 'you're sick.' They identify the type: a cold, a sprain, an allergy. JavaScript errors work the same way. Each error type tells you a different category of problem, which points you toward a different kind of fix.

teach.title: Error Types Are Like Warning Lights on a Dashboard
teach.body: Your car dashboard has different warning lights for different problems -- one for low oil, one for engine temperature, one for tire pressure. Each light tells you a different system needs attention and a different kind of fix. JavaScript error types work the same way: SyntaxError means your grammar needs fixing, TypeError means you mixed up your data types, and each one points to a different repair.

teach.title: The Four Main Error Types
teach.body:
JavaScript has four error types you'll see most often:

1. **SyntaxError** -- your code has a grammar mistake and can't even run
2. **ReferenceError** -- you used a variable name that doesn't exist
3. **TypeError** -- you tried an operation on the wrong kind of value
4. **RangeError** -- a number is outside the allowed range

teach.title: Spotting the Difference
teach.annotation: Each error type points to a different kind of mistake. SyntaxError means broken grammar, ReferenceError means a missing name, TypeError means a mismatched operation.

teach.body: When AI generates code with a TypeError or ReferenceError, knowing the difference means you can tell the AI exactly what to fix instead of starting over. That speed is what turns vibecoding from a hobby into income.
The error type is your first clue. SyntaxError means check your punctuation. ReferenceError means check your variable names. TypeError means you're using a value in a way it doesn't support.

cards.content: A ReferenceError means you used a variable name that doesn't exist.
cards.explanation: ReferenceError happens when JavaScript can't find the variable you're referring to. Common causes: typos in variable names, using a variable before declaring it, or forgetting to import something. Example: console.log(userName) when you named it 'username' (lowercase n).

cards.content: A TypeError means you used the wrong type of quotes around a string.
cards.explanation: TypeError means you tried to do something with a value that doesn't support that operation. For example, calling .map() on a number, or trying to invoke something that isn't a function. Wrong quotes would cause a SyntaxError, not a TypeError.

cards.content: A SyntaxError means JavaScript couldn't even understand your code enough to run it.
cards.explanation: SyntaxError is like a grammar error in English -- JavaScript can't parse what you wrote. Missing brackets, extra commas, forgetting to close a string -- these all prevent the code from running at all. The code never executes; it fails at the parsing stage.

cards.content: TypeError: x is not a function means the variable x doesn't exist.
cards.explanation: If x didn't exist, you'd get a ReferenceError. 'x is not a function' means x DOES exist, but it's not a function -- maybe it's a string, a number, or undefined. A common cause: you imported something incorrectly and it came in as undefined, then you tried to call it with parentheses.

cards.content: RangeError usually means a number is outside the allowed range, like an invalid array length.
cards.explanation: RangeError fires when a value isn't in the set or range of allowed values. Common examples: creating an array with a negative length (new Array(-1)), infinite recursion that blows the call stack, or passing an invalid radix to Number.toString().

cards.content: All JavaScript errors will crash your program and stop it completely.
cards.explanation: Errors only crash the current execution path. In a browser, one error in a click handler doesn't crash the whole page. In Node.js, unhandled errors in async code might not crash the process. And try-catch blocks can handle errors gracefully without crashing anything.
